In [37]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import os
from PIL import Image

def show_images(df, condition, title):
    samples = df[condition].head(5)   # take first 5 matching rows

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))

    for i, row in enumerate(samples.itertuples()):
        img_path = os.path.join("data/train/images", row.image)
        img = Image.open(img_path)

        plt.subplot(1, 5, i + 1)
        plt.imshow(img)
        plt.axis("off")

    plt.suptitle(title)
    plt.show()
In [38]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import os
from PIL import Image

def show_images(t, condition, title):
    samples = t[condition].head(5)

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))

    for i, row in enumerate(samples.itertuples()):
        img_path = os.path.join("data/train/images", row.image)
        img = Image.open(img_path)

        plt.subplot(1, 5, i + 1)
        plt.imshow(img)
        plt.axis("off")

    plt.suptitle(title)
    plt.show()

In [50]:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
from torchvision import models, transforms
from PIL import Image
import pandas as pd
import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [51]:
class TrafficDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, df, img_dir, label_col, transform=None):
        self.df = df.reset_index(drop=True)
        self.img_dir = img_dir
        self.label_col = label_col
        self.transform = transform

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.df)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        row = self.df.iloc[idx]

        frame = f"{int(row['frame']):06d}.jpg"
        img_path = os.path.join(self.img_dir, frame)

        image = Image.open(img_path).convert("RGB")
        label = torch.tensor(row[self.label_col], dtype=torch.float32)

        if self.transform:
            image = self.transform(image)

        return image, label
In [52]:
transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
])
In [53]:
def get_model():
    model = models.resnet18(pretrained=True)
    model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 1)  # binary output
    return model
In [54]:
    def train_model(model, train_loader, val_loader, epochs=5, lr=1e-4):
        
        device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
        model = model.to(device)
    
        criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()
        optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    
        train_losses = []
        val_losses = []
    
        for epoch in range(epochs):
            
            # ---------------- TRAIN ----------------
            model.train()
            total_train_loss = 0
    
            for images, labels in train_loader:
                images, labels = images.to(device), labels.to(device).unsqueeze(1)
    
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                outputs = model(images)
                loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
    
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()
    
                total_train_loss += loss.item()
    
            train_losses.append(total_train_loss / len(train_loader))
    
            # ---------------- VALIDATION ----------------
            model.eval()
            total_val_loss = 0
    
            with torch.no_grad():
                for images, labels in val_loader:
                    images, labels = images.to(device), labels.to(device).unsqueeze(1)
    
                    outputs = model(images)
                    loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
    
                    total_val_loss += loss.item()
    
            val_losses.append(total_val_loss / len(val_loader))
    
            print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: Train Loss={train_losses[-1]:.4f}, Val Loss={val_losses[-1]:.4f}")
    
        return train_losses, val_losses

val_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)  # replace with real split

test_df = pd.read_csv("data/test/labels.csv")
In [67]:
img_dir_test = "data/test/rgb-front"

test_ped = TrafficDataset(test_df, img_dir_test, "has_pedestrian", transform)
test_traffic = TrafficDataset(test_df, img_dir_test, "has_traffic_light", transform)
test_vehicle = TrafficDataset(test_df, img_dir_test, "has_vehicle", transform)

test_loader_ped = DataLoader(test_ped, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
test_loader_traffic = DataLoader(test_traffic, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
test_loader_vehicle = DataLoader(test_vehicle, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
In [68]:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import numpy as np

def evaluate_model(model, test_loader):
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

    model.eval()
    model.to(device)

    y_true = []
    y_pred = []

    with torch.no_grad():
        for images, labels in test_loader:

            images = images.to(device)

            outputs = model(images)
            probs = torch.sigmoid(outputs)

            preds = (probs > 0.5).float().cpu().numpy()

            y_pred.extend(preds.flatten())
            y_true.extend(labels.numpy())

    accuracy = accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
    precision = precision_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)
    recall = recall_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)
    f1 = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)

    return accuracy, precision, recall, f1
In [69]:
ped_results = evaluate_model(ped_model, test_loader_ped)
traffic_results = evaluate_model(traffic_model, test_loader_traffic)
vehicle_results = evaluate_model(vehicle_model, test_loader_vehicle)

print("Pedestrian:", ped_results)
print("Traffic Light:", traffic_results)
print("Vehicle:", vehicle_results)

In [72]:
'''
Analysis:

The Traffic Light model achieved the best overall performance with the highest accuracy, recall, and F1-score.
The Vehicle model also performed very well with high precision and recall.
The Pedestrian model achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, indicating that pedestrian detection is the most challenging task among the three.

2

The Pedestrian model performs the worst with an F1-score of 32.21%, which is significantly lower than the Traffic Light and Vehicle models.

Possible reasons include:

Pedestrians often appear smaller in images compared to vehicles.
Pedestrians can be partially occluded by other objects.
There is high visual variability due to different poses, clothing, and distances from the camera.
The dataset may contain fewer pedestrian examples, causing class imbalance and making learning more difficult.

Therefore, pedestrian detection is a more complex task, which likely explains the lower performance.

3

For all three detection tasks, Recall is the most important metric from a safety perspective.

Pedestrian Detection

Recall is critical because missing a pedestrian (false negative) could lead to serious accidents. It is safer to detect extra pedestrians than to fail to detect a real one.

Vehicle Detection

Recall is important because failing to detect a nearby vehicle could result in unsafe driving decisions, such as collisions or incorrect lane changes.

Traffic Light Detection

Recall is also important because missing a traffic light may cause the system to ignore important traffic regulations and signals.

Conclusion:
In autonomous driving systems, false negatives are generally more dangerous than false positives. Therefore, maximizing Recall is usually more important than maximizing Precision for pedestrian, vehicle, and traffic light detection tasks.
'''
Out[72]:
'\nAnalysis:\n\nThe Traffic Light model achieved the best overall performance with the highest accuracy, recall, and F1-score.\nThe Vehicle model also performed very well with high precision and recall.\nThe Pedestrian model achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, indicating that pedestrian detection is the most challenging task among the three.\n\n2\n\nThe Pedestrian model performs the worst with an F1-score of 32.21%, which is significantly lower than the Traffic Light and Vehicle models.\n\nPossible reasons include:\n\nPedestrians often appear smaller in images compared to vehicles.\nPedestrians can be partially occluded by other objects.\nThere is high visual variability due to different poses, clothing, and distances from the camera.\nThe dataset may contain fewer pedestrian examples, causing class imbalance and making learning more difficult.\n\nTherefore, pedestrian detection is a more complex task, which likely explains the lower performance.\n\n3\n\nFor all three detection tasks, Recall is the most important metric from a safety perspective.\n\nPedestrian Detection\n\nRecall is critical because missing a pedestrian (false negative) could lead to serious accidents. It is safer to detect extra pedestrians than to fail to detect a real one.\n\nVehicle Detection\n\nRecall is important because failing to detect a nearby vehicle could result in unsafe driving decisions, such as collisions or incorrect lane changes.\n\nTraffic Light Detection\n\nRecall is also important because missing a traffic light may cause the system to ignore important traffic regulations and signals.\n\nConclusion:\nIn autonomous driving systems, false negatives are generally more dangerous than false positives. Therefore, maximizing Recall is usually more important than maximizing Precision for pedestrian, vehicle, and traffic light detection tasks.\n'
In [75]:
'''

1. Conditions covered in the training data

The training data mainly contains:

Front-facing RGB traffic scenes
Road environments with pedestrians, vehicles, and traffic lights
Mostly normal lighting conditions (daytime)
Standard urban driving scenes captured from a fixed camera perspective

2. ODD dimensions not covered by the training data

The dataset does not include:

Night-time or low-light conditions
Weather variations such as rain, fog, or snow
Different camera views (side, rear, or aerial)
Diverse environments like rural roads or highways (explicitly separated)
Multiple sensor types (only RGB camera is used)

3. Safety implications of this gap
The model may fail in unseen conditions not present in training data
There is no guarantee of performance under night or adverse weather conditions
The system cannot be safely assumed to generalize beyond the observed dataset
This creates a risk of incorrect detections in real-world deployment

4. Identified ODD gaps
No night-time driving data
No adverse weather conditions
Limited to front-facing camera only
Limited environmental diversity
No variation in sensor types or viewpoints
'''
Out[75]:
'\n\n1. Conditions covered in the training data\n\nThe training data mainly contains:\n\nFront-facing RGB traffic scenes\nRoad environments with pedestrians, vehicles, and traffic lights\nMostly normal lighting conditions (daytime)\nStandard urban driving scenes captured from a fixed camera perspective\n\n2. ODD dimensions not covered by the training data\n\nThe dataset does not include:\n\nNight-time or low-light conditions\nWeather variations such as rain, fog, or snow\nDifferent camera views (side, rear, or aerial)\nDiverse environments like rural roads or highways (explicitly separated)\nMultiple sensor types (only RGB camera is used)\n\n3. Safety implications of this gap\nThe model may fail in unseen conditions not present in training data\nThere is no guarantee of performance under night or adverse weather conditions\nThe system cannot be safely assumed to generalize beyond the observed dataset\nThis creates a risk of incorrect detections in real-world deployment\n\n4. Identified ODD gaps\nNo night-time driving data\nNo adverse weather conditions\nLimited to front-facing camera only\nLimited environmental diversity\nNo variation in sensor types or viewpoints\n'
